mnemonics.title: Remember argc and argv
mnemonics.content: A for ARGument COUNT (integer), V for vector of ARGuments (array of strings). Think: 'C'ount comes first, 'V'ector comes second in (argc, argv)

mnemonics.title: Index Pattern
mnemonics.content: argv[0] = Name, argv[1] = First actual argument. Remember: 0-indexed but program name is position 0, so first user argument is at index 1.

mnemonics.title: Conversion Functions
mnemonics.content: atoi = string TO Integer, atof = string TO Float, atol = string TO Long. All start with 'a' meaning 'ASCII to'.

acronyms.usage: Number of command line arguments including program name
Array containing all command line arguments as strings
Optional third parameter containing environment variables
Library function to parse command-line options/flags

visualTips.visualization: Think of argv as a 1D array: [0]->"./prog" [1]->"hello" [2]->"world" [3]->NULL. Each arrow points to a separate string stored in memory.
argc is like the length of a train, argv is each carriage. argc tells you how many carriages (including engine = program name) are attached.
Index: 0=Program, 1=1st arg, 2=2nd arg. Formula: User argument N is at argv[N]. For Nth user argument (starting from 1), it's at argv[N].

analogies.analogy: Think of argc as the count of items in a shopping list including the list itself, and argv as the actual list items stored as text notes.
Running './prog hello' is like handing a note (program) along with additional notes (arguments) to a clerk (OS) who organizes them.
Converting command line strings to numbers with atoi() is like translating a foreign language document - you extract the numerical meaning from text form.
